Facts.text: Copper coins are minted in Japan for the first time (Traditional Japanese date: August 10, 708)
Anti-Pope Callistus III gives the title of pope to Alexander III
Jacques Pantaleon is elected as Pope Urban IV
Giovanni Battista Cibo elected as Pope Innocent VIII
Gilbert Kennedy, Scottish Earl of Cassilis, roasts the administrator of Crossraguel Abbey, Alan Stewart, over an open fire at Castle Dunure to obtain Abbey lands; it works after two turns of the spit
Battle of Surat, India: English fleet defeats Portuguese
Warsaw falls without resistance to a small force under the command of Charles X Gustav of Sweden during the Deluge
Adriaen Pieck and Gerrit de Ferry patent wooden firespout in Amsterdam
British troops occupy Menorca and Sardinia
Raid in Haverhill, Massachusetts, by French, Algonquin, and Abenaki forces kills or captures 40 people
English writer Edmond Hoyle publishes his "Short Treatise on the Game of Whist"
Frederick II of Prussia leads an Anglo-Prussian alliance into Saxony, beginning the Seven Years' War
New Jersey Legislature forms the first Indian reservation
Americans withdraw from Manhattan to Westchester
British man o' war HMS Royal George capsizes at Spithead, off Hampshire, England, killing more than 800 crew members and visitors

Shays' Rebellion, an armed uprising in Springfield and Worcester, Massachusetts, by citizens in opposition to the government's increased efforts to collect taxes on both individuals and their businesses
Slaves in the French colony of Saint-Domingue (Haiti) are freed
Portugal recognizes the independence of its former colony Brazil
United Kingdom and China sign the Treaty of Nanking, ending the First Opium War
First white-Indian lacrosse game in Montreal; Indigenous team wins
Self-governing windmill patented by American inventor Daniel Halladay

Battle of Hatteras Inlet Batteries: Union Army and Navy in a combined operation capture Fort Hatteras off North Carolina, resulting in Union domination of the strategically important North Carolina Sounds
British astronomer William Huggins discovers chemical composition of nebulae
Emperor Meiji orders the abolition of the han system and the establishment of prefectures as local centers of administration (Traditional Japanese date: July 14, 1871)
Phillies' Charlie Ferguson no-hits Providence 1-0
First American international professional lawn tennis contest in Newport, Rhode Island
Northern Rugby Union is formed at the George Hotel in Huddersfield, England
Chop suey is supposedly invented in NYC by the chef of visiting Chinese diplomat Li Hongzhang
The Goodyear tire company founded
Gaetano Bresci, the assassin of King Umberto I of Italy, is tried and sentenced to life imprisonment; he commits suicide in jail on May 22, 1901
The Finance Minister, Count Witte, is dismissed in what is seen as a victory for those in Russia who want their country to expand into Manchuria and Korea in defiance of Japan
The Quebec Bridge over St Lawrence River collapses during construction, killing 75 workers
New York City gives a ticker tape parade to returning US Olympians from London
Hubert Latham of France sets a world airplane altitude record of 155 meters
Japan changes Korea's name to Chōsen and appoints a governor-general to rule its new colony
Ishi, considered the last Native American to make contact with European Americans, emerges from the wilderness of northeastern California
Pieter Cort Van de Linden forms Dutch government
Arizonian is the first vessel to arrive in San Francisco via the Panama Canal
Battle of St. Quentin: French counterattack led by General Lanrezac
New Zealand forces capture German Samoa
Congress creates US Naval Reserve
US Congress accepts Jones Act: Philippines independence
Canadian Parliament passes the Military Service Act, which is opposed by many French Canadians from Québec and farmers across the country
An American sweep of the medals in the men's 100 m freestyle at the Antwerp Olympics; Duke Kahanamoku sets a world record of 1:00.4 in defending his 1912 gold medal
An American sweep of the medals in the women's 3 m springboard diving event at the Antwerp Olympics as Aileen Riggin wins gold ahead of teammates Helen Wainwright and Thelma Payne
Swedish swimmer Håkan Malmrot completes the breaststroke double at the Antwerp Olympics, beating teammate Thor Henning in the 200 m and also beats Henning for gold in the 400m
Germany's Reichstag approves the Dawes Plan, which sought to solve the WWI reparations problem
After a night on the town,Babe Ruthshows up late for batting practice, and Miller Huggins suspends Ruth and imposes a $5,000 fine on him
The last 36 remaining inhabitants of St Kilda are voluntarily evacuated to other parts of Scotland
International Anti-War Committee forms in Amsterdam
United Cigar Stores shuts 800 shops
Second NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Chicago Bears 5, All-Stars 0 (77,450 attendees)
MLB Philadelphia A's Bob Johnson is the second player to achieve 6 RBIs in an inning (first inning)
Seventh NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Green Bay 45, All-Stars 28 (84,567 attendees)
German Einsatzkommando kills 1,469 Jewish children in Russia
Danish Navy scuttles its warships so as not to be taken by Germany
15,000 American troops liberate Paris and march down the Champs-Élysées
Anti-German rebellion in Slovakia
British liberate Hong Kong from Japan
Constantine Tsaldaris succeeds Maximos as Greek premier
Soviet Union secretly performs its first successful nuclear weapons test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
International Olympic Committee votes to admit West Germany and Japan in 1952
New York premiere of history-based film "The Miracle of Our Lady of Fatima"
KHSL TV channel 12 in Chico, CA (CBS) begins broadcasting
USSR explodes its first hydrogen bomb
San Francisco International Airport (SFO) opens
French government sends troops to Cyprus during Suez Crisis
US Air Force Academy's permanent site opens in Colorado Springs, Colorado

Australian swimmerDawn Fraserwins the women's 100 m freestyle gold medal at the Rome Olympics, retaining the title won in Melbourne 1956, and will win an unprecedented third consecutive 100 m gold in Tokyo 1964

Italy wins the cycling team pursuit, and Sante Gaiardoni takes the sprint gold medal at the Rome Olympics; Italy wins all four track events and the road team time trial to thoroughly dominate the sport
Jordanian Premier Hazza' al-Majali is fatally injured in a bomb attack
Some provisions of Kuwaiti constitution are suspended
US U-2 flight sees SAM launch pads in Cuba
Twins playerHarmon Killebrewhits a home run off Senator Pete Burnside in a doubleheader
Gemini V returns to Earth after 120 orbits and a record 7 days, 22 hours, 55 minutes, and 14 seconds
San Francisco Giants'Willie Mayssets an NL record for home runs in a month with his 17th in August
Dutch Interior Minister Smallenbroek resigns after driving drunk
The Beatles' last public concert takes place before a crowd of 25,000, with 7,000 unsold seats, at Candlestick Park in San Francisco, California
Final TV episode of "The Fugitive" starring David Janssen is watched by 78 million people
Yankees' longest day: Red Sox win the first game 2-1 in 9 innings, and Yankees win the second game in 20 innings, 4-3, totaling 8 hours and 19 minutes
Joe Pepitonequits NY Yankees after being fined $500 for leaving the bench
KYUS TV channel 3 in Miles City, MT (ABC/NBC) begins broadcasting
Black Panthers confront police in Philadelphia; one officer is killed
Sandy Neilson wins the women's 100 m freestyle gold medal in 58.59 seconds at the Munich Olympics, beating fellow American Shirley Babashoff andShane Gouldof Australia
SF Giant Jim Barr retires the first 20 batters he faces, added to the last 21 he retired 6 days earlier for a record 41 in a row
USSR performs an underground nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
Star in Cygnus goes nova, becoming the fourth brightest in the sky
USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
USTA National Tennis Center opens in Flushing Meadows, New York
Great Britain performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Sheridan Broadcasting Corporation purchases Mutual Black Network
Phillies minor leaguer Jeff Stone steals a professional baseball record 121st base en route to 122 (Spartanburg, South Atlantic League)
38°F (3.33°C) is the lowest temperature ever recorded in the month of August in Cleveland, Ohio
MLB Kansas City Royals infielderGeorge Brettgets his 1,500th hit
The synthetic chemical element Meitnerium, atomic number 109, is first synthesized at the Gesellschaft für Schwerionenforschung in Darmstadt, Germany
Atlantis moves to the launch pad for the 51-J mission
New York Yankees Don Baylor is hit by a pitch for a record 190th time
Heike Drechsler of East Germany ties the world women's 200 m record (21.71 seconds)
Houston Astros MLB playerNolan Ryansurpasses the 200-strikeout barrier for a record 11th time
Portuguese athleteRosa Motawins the Women's Marathon at the World Championships in Rome in 2 hours, 25 minutes, and 17 seconds
Macy's Tap-o-Mania sets a Guinness World Record for tap dancing, with 4,497 participants dancing to "There's No Business Like Show Business"
USSR launches Soyuz TM-6, including Valery Polyakov, Vladimir Lyakhov, and the first Afghan cosmonaut Abdul Ahad Mohmand, to the Mir station
C-5 transport plane crashes at Ramstein Air Base, Germany, killing 13
JFK Jr. wins his first battle as an attorney
USSR suspends Communist Party activity
Braves' Charlie Leibrandt records his 1,000th strikeout. He decides to keep the ball by rolling it to the dugout, allowing Ricky Jordan to take second base on an error
Irish rock bandU2plays the first of two sold-out nights at Yankee Stadium in the Bronx, NYC
Randy Myers blows his sixth save of the season, the fifth time he's blown a potential win for Greg Harris
John Harris wins the 93rd US Golf Amateur Championship
Vnukovo Airlines Flight 2801, operated by a Tupolev Tu-154, crashes into a mountain on the Arctic island of Spitsbergen, killing all 141 on board
Netflix is founded by Marc Randolph and Reed Hastings in Scotts Valley, California, as an online DVD rental business
Goodwill Games V opens in Brisbane, Australia, for the final edition of the event
Ayatollah Sayed Mohammed Baqir al-Hakim, the Shia Muslim leader in Iraq, is assassinated in a terrorist bombing, along with nearly 100 worshippers as they leave a mosque in Najaf
Skype Technologies releases its pioneering video-conferencing software
28th Summer Olympic Games close at the Olympic Stadium in Athens, Greece

Athens Olympic marathon is run on the same route as the 1896 Games, starting at the site of the Battle of Marathon to the Panathenaic Stadium in Athens; Stefano Baldini of Italy wins men's gold in 2:10:55
Brazil wins the men's volleyball gold medal with a 3-1 victory over Italy at the Athens Olympics
Future undisputed world light heavyweight boxing champion Andre Ward of the US wins the light heavyweight gold medal at the Athens Olympics, defeating Magomed Aripgadjiev of Belarus on points
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf at Reserve Vineyards & GC: Craig Stadler wins his final major event by one stroke overAllen Doyleand Jerry Pate

Six AGM-129 ACM cruise missiles, each loaded with a W80-1 variable yield nuclear warhead, are mistakenly loaded onto a B-52H heavy bomber at Minot Air Force Base in North Dakota and flown to Barksdale Air Force Base in Louisiana
British BBC television series "Last of the Summer Wine" airs its final episode, ending after 31 seasons as the longest-running sitcom ever
Banana Spider venom is found to be effective in relieving erectile dysfunction
Georgian hostage crisis results in 3 police officers and 10 militants being killed
Operation Eagle, undertaken by the Egyptian Army, results in the deaths of 11 suspected terrorists and the arrest of another 23
41 people are killed and 33 are injured in a bus accident in Kenya
Riots break out in Guinea following rumors that health workers are deliberately transmitting the Ebola virus to locals
Senegal is the fifth country hit by Ebola
Italian coastguard says 6,500 migrants are rescued at sea in 40 separate incidents in one day off the coast of Sabratha, Libya
Hurricane Harvey sets a rainfall record of 51.88 inches in Cedar Bayou from a tropical cyclone in the continental US, according to the US National Weather Service
Monsoon rains in Mumbai cause chaos, closing schools and airports
Former Scottish First Minister Alex Salmond resigns from the Scottish National Party amid sexual abuse allegations
Germany returns the human remains of Namibian Herero and Nama people killed during the 1904-08 genocide at a church service in Berlin
Discovery of world's largest child sacrifice site is announced by archaeologists with 227 victims from the Chimú culture in Huanchaco, Peru
Scientists announce there is no single "gay" gene, with genetics accounting for at most 25% of same-sex behavior in a study published in "Science"
Hurricane Ida makes landfall as a Category 4 storm near Port Fourchon, Louisiana, on the anniversary of Hurricane Katrina
Missile and drone attack on al-Anad airbase in southern Yemen kills at least 30 soldiers, one of the deadliest attacks in recent years
St. Louis Cardinals sluggerAlbert Pujolshits career home run 694 off major league record 450th different pitcher in a 13-4 win over the Reds in Cincinnati
Worst violence in Iraqi capital Baghdad in years with 30 killed and 700 injured after Shia leader Muqtada al-Sadr announces withdrawal from politics
Greek wildfire north of the city of Alexandroupoli is declared the EU's largest ever recorded, having burned 310 sq miles (810 sq km) and killed 20 people
Parasite worm, alive and wriggling, found in a woman's brain for the first time in Canberra, Australia
Typhoon Shanshan makes landfall in Japan on the southern island of Kyushu, with the country issuing its highest level 5 alert
A 21-year-old delivery driver is run over and killed by a police vehicle in Jakarta, Indonesia, during ongoing protests, increasing violence across the country, with at least three more dead
Andriy Parubiy, former speaker of the Ukrainian parliament, is shot dead in Lviv, Ukraine, sparking a manhunt for the suspect
Battle of Winchelsea (or Les Espagnols sur Mer): The English naval fleet under KingEdward IIIdefeats a Castilian fleet of 40 ships
Peace Treaty of Picquigny: KingLouis XIbuys English contacts
Battle of Mohács: In a decisive battle, the Kingdom of Hungary is conquered by the Ottoman Empire led bySuleiman the Magnificent
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vdeprives the city Ghent of its definitive rights and privileges
KingLouis XIVof France andVictor Amadeus II, Duke of Savoy, sign the Peace of Turin, a turning point in the War of the League of Augsburg
Domitila de Castromeets her long-term lover DomPedro I, then Prince of Portugal and later Emperor of Brazil, for the first time in São Paulo
Michael Faradaydemonstrates the first electric transformer
Jacob and WilhelmGrimmannounce their intention to publish a German Dictionary, eventually completed in 1961 after 123 years
Battle of Aspromonte: Italian royal forces defeat rebels in southern Italy and take their leaderGiuseppe Garibaldiprisoner
Australia beats England by 7 runs in a match dubbed the "Death of English cricket" as Australian fast bowlerFred Spofforthtakes 14-90 (7-46 and 7-44)
The first Carnegie library, financed by industrialistAndrew Carnegie, opens in Dunfermline, Scotland, as the first of 2,509 libraries built around the world
Boxing's first heavyweight title fight with 3-ounce gloves and 3-minute rounds is fought between American boxersJohn L. Sullivanand Dominick McCaffrey in Cincinnati, Ohio
Gottlieb Daimlerreceives a German patent for a motorcycle
Pierre Savorgnan de Brazzaleaves Brazzaville
Fourth day of the Battle ofTannenberg(WWI): Russian Second Army panics, General Martos is caught
GeneralPaul von Hindenburgreplaces Erich von Falkenhayn as German Chief of Staff
WWI: Bapaume, France, is retaken from the Germans in the Second Battle of Bapaume by British, Australian, New Zealand, andCanadian Corpsduring the Hundred Days Offensive

Ethelda Bleibtreywins her third gold medal at the Antwerp Olympics, teaming with Irene Guest, Frances Schroth, and Margaret Woodbridge in the American 4 x 100 m freestyle relay team, setting a world record of 5:11.6
AviatorAnne Morrow Lindberghmakes her first solo flight
Chaim Weizmanninforms England that Palestinian Jews will fight in WWII
WWII: USGeneral MacArthurnamed Supreme Commander of Allied Powers in Japan
B.R. Ambedkaris appointed chairman of the drafting committee for the Indian Constitution
US Democratic SenatorStrom Thurmondends the longest-ever U.S. filibuster of 24 hours and 18 minutes against the Civil Rights Act
Cliff Richardand the Drifters release their debut single "Move It," credited as the first British rock 'n' roll song
GuitaristGeorge Harrison(15) joins The Quarrymen, a skiffle band that later becomes The Beatles
Swedish canoeistsGert Fredrikssonand Sven-Olov Sjödelius win the K2-1,000 m in 3:34.73 at the Rome Olympics; Fredriksson's sixth gold medal in four Olympic Games
OnElston HowardNight, NY Yankees'Mickey Mantleties Babe Ruth's career strikeout record of 1,330
Anne McCaffreymakes history as the first woman to win a Hugo Award for Best Novella with her work "Weyr Search" (Dragonflight)
Democrats nominateHubert Humphreyfor US President in Chicago
Iggy Popreleases his second album "Lust for Life," a collaboration withDavid Bowie
MLB St. Louis Cardinals'Lou BrockeclipsesTy Cobb's 49-year-old career stolen bases record at 893 as the Padres win 4-3
Steve Miller's single "Abracadabra" hits #1
Moroccan KingHassan IIsigns a unity treaty with Libya
Saddam Husseindeclares that America can't defeat Iraq

Kazakh PresidentNursultan Nazarbayevsigns a decree to close the Semipalatinsk test site, the location of 456 Soviet nuclear tests from 1949 to 1989, following protests by the Nevada-Semipalatinsk anti-nuclear movement
British bandOasisrelease their debut album "Definitely Maybe," which becomes the fastest-selling album in the UK
German Ferrari driverMichael Schumacherclinches his fifth consecutive F1 World Drivers' Championship with a second place finish in the Belgian F1 Grand Prix at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps
Cristiano Ronaldomakes his debut for Real Madrid, scoring a penalty in their 3-2 win over Deportivo La Coruña
62nd Emmy Awards: Mad Men,Modern Family,Bryan Cranston, and Kyra Sedgwick win
"Tha Carter IV" 9th studio album byLil Wayneis released
ActorGary Siniseis named an honorary Marine by the Commandant of the Marine Corps
US PresidentDonald Trumpvisits flood-affected Texas
John McCainis only the third person to lie in state at the Arizona State Capitol Rotunda in Phoenix
Russian PresidentVladimir Putinannounces new retirement ages in a TV address amid protests: 60 for women and 65 for men
Star quarterbackAaron Rodgerssigns an NFL record contract extension with the Green Bay Packers: 4 years worth $134 million, rising to a possible $180 million with a record $103 million in guarantees
Elon Muskunveils a pig named Gertrude with a coin-sized computer in her brain as part of his Neuralink start-up creating a brain-to-machine interface
Atahualpa, the last Sapa Inca Emperor, is suspected to have been buried in northern Peru or in Ecuador
Britain's first Factory Act becomes law to regulate the labor of children and young persons in the mills and factories of the United Kingdom
Second Battle of Bull Run begins in Manassas, Virginia, leading to a Confederate victory in the US Civil War
The US Department of the Treasury forms the division now known as the US Bureau of Engraving and Printing to print newly issued paper currency
Seismic sea waves created by the Krakatoa eruption cause a rise in the English Channel 32 hours after the explosion
Glenn Curtiss of the USA wins the world's first air race, held in Rheims, France, on the final day of the Grande Semaine d'Aviation
German airshipGraf Zeppelinends a round-the-world flight

Hurricane Katrina makes its second and third landfalls as a Category 3 hurricane, devastating much of the Gulf Coast from Louisiana to the Florida Panhandle, killing more than 1,836 people and causing over $115 billion in damage
John of Artois, Count of Eu, French nobleman and soldier captured at the Battle of Poitiers (place of birth unknown), born in France (d. 1387)[1]
Janus Pannonius, Hungarian poet and translator, born in Čazma, Croatia (d. 1472)
Sassoferrato [Giovanni B Salvi], Italian painter (Madonna), born in Sassoferrato, Marche, Italy (d. 1685)
Jean-Baptiste Colbert, French statesman (First Minister of State, 1661-83; Minister of the Navy, 1689-83), born in Reims, France (d. 1683)
John Granville, 1st Earl of Bath, English royalist statesman, born in Kilkhampton, Cornwall (d. 1701)
Wilhelm Hieronymus Pachelbel, German composer, baptized in Erfurt, Electorate of Mainz, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1764)  [O.S. date 8-19]
Charles Townshend, English politician, 3rd Viscount of Townshend, born in Raynham Hall, Norfolk (d. 1767)
Maria Anna Sophia of Saxony, Electress of Bavaria, born in Dresden Castle, Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1797)
Heinrich Graf von Bellegarde, Austrian field marshal and statesman, born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1845)
Jan Śniadecki, Polish mathematician and astronomer, born in Żnin, Poland (d. 1830)
Raphael G. Kiesewetter, Austrian bureaucrat and musicologist (Arab Music), born in Holleschau, Margraviate of Moravia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1850)
Hyacinth [Nikita Yakovlevich Bichurin], founder of Sinology in Russia, born in Akulevo, Russia (d. 1853)
Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres, French painter (Valpincon Bather), born in Montauban, France (d. 1867)
Frederick Maurice, English theologian of the Church of England (Christian Socialism), and author, born in Normanston, Suffolk, England (d. 1872)
Hermann Schulze-Delitzsch, German politician and reformer, born in Delitzsch, Saxony (d. 1883)
Oliver Wendell Holmes, Sr., American physician and author (Old Ironsides), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1894)
Juan Bautista Alberdi, Argentina political theorist and writer (Crime of War), born in San Miguel de Tucumán, Río de la Plata (d. 1884)

Henry Bergh, American social reformer who founded the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (ASPCA) and co-founded the New York Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NYSPCC), born in New York City (d. 1888)
Anna Ella Carroll, American politician and civil war writer (Reconstruction), born in Pocomoke City, Maryland (d. 1894)
Louis Laurent Gabriel de Mortillet, French anthropologist who was the first to organize and classify Stone Age cultures into a chronological sequence of epochs, born in Meylan, France (d. 1898)

Alfred Shaw, English cricket medium/spin bowler (7 Tests; bowled first ball in Test cricket; first to take 5 wickets in a Test innings; Nottinghamshire CCC, MCC), born in Burton Joyce, England (d. 1907)
David B. Hill, American politician and Governor of New York (1885-91), born in Montour Falls, New York (d. 1910)
Edward Carpenter, English poet and philosopher (Civilisation, Its Cause and Cure), born in Hove, Sussex, England (d. 1929)
Titus van Asch van Wijck, Dutch governor Suriname (1891-97), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1902)
Joseph Jacobs, Australian folklorist, historian, and literary critic (English Fairy Tales, More English Fairy Tales), born in Sydney, Colony of New South Wales (d. 1916)
Byron G. Harlan, American balladeer ("Wait 'Till the Sun Shines, Nellie"), and comic minstrel singer (Collins & Harlan), born in Kansas (d. 1936)
Albert Lebrun, President of France (1932-40), born in Mercy-le-Haut, France (d. 1950)
Charles F. Kettering, American inventor (auto self-starter), born in Loudenville, Ohio (d. 1958)
Kim Gu, President of the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea (1939-48), born in Haeju, Hwanghae, Korea (d. 1949)
Dudley Pound, British admiral of the fleet and 1st Sea Lord (Jutland, WW II), born in Ventnor, Isle of Wight, England (d. 1943)
Marie-Louise Meilleur, oldest French-Canadian supercentenarian (117 years, 230 days), born in Kamouraska, Quebec (d. 1998)
Edvin Kallstenius, Swedish composer and librarian, born in Filipstad, Sweden (d. 1967)
Valery-Nicolas Larbaud, French novelist and translator (Enfantines), born in Vichy, France (d. 1957)
Ehm Welk [Thomas Trimm], German writer, born in Biesenbrow, Brandenburg (d. 1966)
Michael Chekhov, Russian-American actor (Abie's Irish Rose, Rhapsody), born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1955)
E. Franklin Frazier, American sociologist and author (The Negro Family in the United States), born in Baltimore (d. 1962)
Preston Sturges [Edmund Biden], American director and screenwriter (Sullivan's Travels), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1959)
George Macready, American actor (Martin Peyton-Peyton Place), born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 1973)
Lyman Lemnitzer, United States Army Marine Corps general (WWII), born in Honesdale, Pennsylvania (d. 1988)
Oscar Nitzchke, German architect (Alcoa building, Centre Pompidou), born in Altona, Hamburg, Germany (d. 1991)
Aurel Joliat, Canadian ice hockey player, born in Ottawa, Canada (d. 1986)
Ernst Kreuder, German writer (The Attic Pretenders), born in Zeitz, Germany (d. 1972)
Werner Forssmann, German urologist (Nobel 1956), born in Berlin (d. 1979)
Arndt Pekurinen, Finnish pacifist, executed without a trial for his beliefs, born in Juva, Finland (d. 1941)
Dhyan Chand, Indian field hockey player (Olympic gold 1928, 1932, 1936; birthday 29/8 celebrated as India's National Sports Day), born in Allahabad, India (d. 1979)
Lan Adomián, Russian-American-Mexican composer, and Spanish Civil War fighter (Lincoln Brigade), born in Moghiler-Podolsk, Russia (d. 1979)
Martinus A. Jansen, Bishop of Rotterdam (1956-70), born in Amsterdam (d. 1983)
Joe Sawyer [Sauers], Canadian actor (Adventures of Rin Tin Tin - "Sgt. Biff O'Hara"), born in Guelph, Ontario (d. 1980)
Joseph Wechsberg, Jewish-Czech writer (Looking For a Bluebird), born in Ostrava, Moravia, Czechoslovakia (d. 1983)
Lurene Tuttle, American actress (Father of Bride, Julia), born in Pleasant Lake, Indiana (d. 1986)[1]
Roy Reuther, American labor leader, born in Wheeling, West Virginia (d. 1968)
Georges Loinger, French resistance fighter (WWII), born in Strasbourg, France (d. 2018)
Robert Pineda-Duque, Colombian composer, born in El Santuario, Colombia (d. 1977)
Barry Sullivan, American actor (Duffy's Tavern, Man Called X, Tall Man), born in New York City (d. 1994)
Emil Schumacher, German painter (Tangible Objects), born in Hagen, Germany (d. 1999)
Sohn Kee-chung, Korean athlete and long-distance runner, born in Sinuiju, North Korea (d. 2002)
Wolfgang Suschitzky, Austrian-Polish cinematographer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2016)
Len Butterfield, New Zealand cricketer (NZ all-rounder, Test), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 1999)
Denys Hay, British historian, born in Newcastle upon Tyne (d. 1994)
Nathan Pritikin, American nutritionist and inventor, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1985)
George Montgomery, American actor (Battle of Bulge, Hallucination), born in Brady, Montana (d. 2000)
Leo Horn, Dutch soccer referee, columnist and businessman, born in Sittard, Netherlands (d. 1995)
Isabel Sanford, American actress (Louise-Jeffersons/All in the Family), born in New York City (d. 2004)
John King, Baron King of Wartnaby, British businessman and CEO of British Airways after its privatization, born in Brentford, Middlesex, England (d. 2005)
Jelle Zijlstra, Dutch economist, politician (Prime Minister, 1966-67), and President of Netherlands Bank (1967-82), born in Oosterbierum, Netherlands (d. 2001)
Sono Osato, American Japanese ballet dancer, born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 2018)
Charles White, American actor (Airport 95, Serpico), born in Perth Amboy, New Jersey (d. 2005)
Joseph Lesniewski, American Easy Company, soldier in 101st Airborne, born in Erie, Pennsylvania (d. 2012)
Wendell Scott, American auto racer (first black driver to win a race at NASCAR's premier level [Jacksonville 200 1963]), born in Danville, Virginia (d. 1990)
Emile Habibi, Palestinian Israeli writer (The Secret Life of Saeed the Pessoptimist), born in Haifa (d. 1996)
Mr. Blackwell, American fashion critic, born in Brooklyn Heights, New York (d. 2008)
Yorgos Sicilianos, Greek composer, born in Athens, Greece (d. 2005)
Lester Trimble, American composer, music critic and educator, born in Bangor, Wisconsin (d. 1986)
Marmaduke Hussey, Baron Hussey of North Bradley and Chairman of the Board of Governors of BBC (1986-96), born in Surrey (d. 2006)
Reinier Braams, Dutch biophysicist and politician, born in 's-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands (d. 2001)
Billy Myles [Nobles], American R&B songwriter and singer ("(You Were Made for) All My Love", "Have You Ever Loved A Woman"), born in Greenville, North Carolina (d. 2005)
Consuelo Velázquez, Mexican songwriter, born in Ciudad Guzmán, Mexico (d. 2005)
Dinah Washington [Ruth Lee Jones], American jazz, blues, pop, and R&B singer ("What a Diff'rence a Day Makes": "September In The Rain"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1963)
James W. Wood, American astronaut (Dynasoar), born in Paragould, Arkansas (d. 1990)
Loyd Gentry, American thoroughbred trainer (Kentucky Derby 1967 Proud Clarion), born in Covington, Kentucky (d. 2012)
Betty Lynn, American actress (The Andy Griffith Show - "Thelma Lou"), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2021)
Donn Fendler, American author and public speaker, who survived a week in the wilderness aged 12, born in Rye, New York (d. 2016)[1]
María Dolores Pradera, Spanish melodic singer and actress, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 2018)
Marion Williams, American gospel singer (Stars of Faith), born in Miami, Florida (d. 1994)
Charles Gray, British actor (Clay-Rawhide, Diamonds Are Forever, The Rocky Horror Picture Show), born in Bournemouth, Hampshire (d. 2000)
Thomas Stewart, American bass-baritone (La Roche Capriccio), born in San Saba, Texas (d. 2006)
Thom Gunn, British poet (The Wound), born in Kent, England (d. 2004)
Jacques Bouchard, Canadian advertising executive, born in Montreal, Québec, Canada (d. 2006)
Lise Payette, Quebec politician, writer and columnist, born in Verdun, Quebec, Canada (d. 2018)
Stelios Kazantzidis, Greek singer, born in Nea Ionia, Athens, Greece (d. 2001)
Arnold Koller, Swiss politician (member of Swiss Federal Councilor), born in St. Gallen, Switzerland
Jehan Sadat (née Safwat Raouf), Egyptian human rights activist, widow of Anwar Sadat and 1st Lady of Egypt (1970-81), born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 2021)[1]
Ramses Shaffy, Dutch singer and cabaret actor (Zaak MP, Liefdesbekentenissen), born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France (d. 2009)
